primer:
## Why C++11 is a turning point
C++11 was the first major revision in over a decade and reshaped idioms — **move semantics, `auto`, lambdas, range-based for, smart pointers, `nullptr`, `constexpr`, and a standard threading + memory model** — making code safer, faster and terser. It is what people mean by 'modern C++'.

## C++11 features (and what they replaced)
**`auto`** deduces a variable's type, replacing verbose names like `std::map<int,std::string>::iterator it = m.begin();` with `auto it = m.begin();` (can't silently mismatch the real type). The **range-based for** `for (const auto& x : v)` replaced index/iterator boilerplate (`for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i)`), removing off-by-one errors; `const auto&` reads without copying or mutating.

**Move semantics** add the ability to transfer a resource cheaply instead of deep-copying: rvalue references (`T&&`) and move constructors let returning a big `vector` or storing a `unique_ptr` steal the buffer/ownership — a big performance win and what enables move-only types.

**`nullptr`** is a type-safe null (`std::nullptr_t`) that only converts to pointers, removing the overload ambiguity of `NULL`/`0` (which are just the integer 0). **Smart pointers** `std::unique_ptr`/`std::shared_ptr` (+ `weak_ptr`) replaced raw `new`/`delete` ownership and the broken `std::auto_ptr`, giving automatic, exception-safe, leak-free RAII ownership.

**`constexpr`** marks a variable/function as compile-time-evaluable, usable where a constant is required (array sizes, template args); C++11 constexpr functions were limited to a single `return`. **Threading**: `std::thread`, `std::mutex`/locks, `std::atomic`, `std::condition_variable`, `std::future`/`std::async`, and a defined memory model replaced platform-specific APIs (pthreads, Win32).

C++11 lets you **`= default;`** (keep the compiler's version) and **`= delete;`** (forbid a function, e.g. `Type(const Type&) = delete;` to make a type non-copyable). And **lambdas** let you pass inline callables to `<algorithm>` for the first time, capturing local state — before, you wrote a separate functor struct or free function for, e.g., `std::sort`'s comparator.

## C++14 polish
**`std::make_unique<T>(args)`** — the factory C++11 forgot (it shipped only `make_shared`); creates a `unique_ptr` without a visible `new`, exception-safe, and is now the recommended way. **Generic lambdas** use `auto` parameters (`[](auto a, auto b){ return a + b; }`), making them templated callables that deduce each type independently. C++14 also **relaxed constexpr functions** to allow loops, local variables and multiple statements.

## Exercises
Modernise a C++03 iterator loop to `for (const auto& x : v) { std::cout << x; }` (range-for + `auto`). Replace `Widget* w = new Widget(1, 2); ... delete w;` with `auto w = std::make_unique<Widget>(1, 2);` — no raw `new`/`delete`, exception-safe, auto-freed at scope exit.
name: C++ — Modern: C++11 & C++14
description: What the C++11 and C++14 standards introduced and why each feature beats the pre-C++11 way: auto, range-for, lambdas, move semantics, nullptr, smart pointers, constexpr, threads, and the C++14 polish (generic lambdas, make_unique).

cards.term: Why is C++11 considered a turning point ('modern C++')?
cards.definition: C++11 was the first major revision in over a decade and reshaped idioms: move semantics, `auto`, lambdas, range-based for, smart pointers, `nullptr`, `constexpr`, and a standard threading and memory model. It made code safer (RAII smart pointers), faster (moves) and terser (auto/lambdas).
cards.summary: First big modern revision: moves, auto, lambdas, smart pointers, threads.

cards.term: What did `auto` change versus the old way (C++11)?
cards.definition: Before, you spelled every type in full, e.g. `std::map<int,std::string>::iterator it = m.begin();`. With `auto it = m.begin();` the compiler deduces it — shorter, and it cannot silently mismatch the real type. Pairs perfectly with `auto` in range-for loops.
cards.summary: Deduces the type, replacing verbose explicit type names.

cards.term: What did the range-based for loop replace (C++11)?
cards.definition: It replaced index-or-iterator boilerplate. Old: `for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) use(v[i]);` or an explicit iterator loop. New: `for (const auto& x : v) use(x);` — no index, no off-by-one, works on any range with `begin`/`end`.
cards.summary: `for (const auto& x : v)` replaces index/iterator loops.

cards.term: What did move semantics (C++11) add over copying?
cards.sub: A) the ability to transfer a resource cheaply instead of deep-copying it    B) faster copies    C) automatic garbage collection    D) implicit pointer arithmetic
cards.definition: Correct: A) the ability to transfer a resource cheaply instead of deep-copying it. Rvalue references (`T&&`) and move constructors let returning a big `vector` or storing a `unique_ptr` steal the internal buffer/ownership rather than duplicate it — a large performance win and what enables move-only types.

`NULL` is usually just `0`, an integer, so it can be ambiguous in overload resolution (does `f(0)` call `f(int)` or `f(char*)`?). `nullptr` has its own type `std::nullptr_t` that only converts to pointer types, removing the ambiguity and reading clearly as 'a null pointer'.
cards.summary: Type-safe null that can't be mistaken for the integer 0.

cards.term: What smart pointers did C++11 standardise, and what did they replace?
cards.definition: `std::unique_ptr` and `std::shared_ptr` (plus `weak_ptr`) replaced raw `new`/`delete` ownership and the broken, copy-unsafe `std::auto_ptr`. They give automatic, exception-safe, leak-free RAII ownership — the headline safety improvement of C++11.
cards.summary: unique_ptr/shared_ptr/weak_ptr replaced raw new/delete and auto_ptr.

cards.definition: It marks a variable or function as evaluable at compile time, so its result can be used where a constant is required (array sizes, template args) and the work is done by the compiler, not at runtime. C++11 constexpr functions were restricted to a single return; later standards relaxed this.
cards.summary: Compile-time evaluation of constants and functions.

cards.term: What threading support did C++11 add?
cards.definition: A standard, portable concurrency library: `std::thread`, `std::mutex` and locks, `std::atomic`, `std::condition_variable`, `std::future`/`std::async`, and a defined memory model. Before, you used platform-specific APIs (pthreads, Win32); now concurrency is in the language.
cards.summary: std::thread, mutex, atomic, future/async + a memory model.

cards.term: C++11 lets you default or delete special members explicitly with `= default;` and {{= delete}}; the latter forbids a function (e.g. to make a type non-copyable).
cards.summary: = default keeps the compiler version; = delete forbids the function.
cards.definition: `Type(const Type&) = delete;` makes copying a compile error — the modern, clear way to express non-copyable, replacing the old trick of declaring a private copy constructor with no body.

cards.term: C++11 lambdas allowed C++ to pass inline callables to STL algorithms ergonomically for the first time.
cards.summary: True — before, you wrote separate function objects (functors) or free functions.
cards.definition: True. Pre-C++11 you passed a hand-written functor struct or a free function to `std::sort`'s comparator. C++11 lambdas let you write the callable inline at the call site, capturing local state, which made `<algorithm>` far more pleasant to use.

cards.term: What was the headline addition in C++14 for smart pointers?
cards.definition: `std::make_unique<T>(args)`. C++11 shipped `make_shared` but forgot `make_unique`; C++14 added it, so you can create a `unique_ptr` without a visible `new` and with exception safety. It became the recommended way to construct `unique_ptr`.
cards.summary: std::make_unique — the missing factory for unique_ptr.

cards.sub: A) lambdas whose parameters use `auto`, making them templated callables    B) lambdas with no captures    C) lambdas that must return void    D) lambdas usable only at namespace scope
cards.definition: Correct: A) lambdas whose parameters use `auto`, making them templated callables. `[](auto a, auto b){ return a + b; }` works for any types supporting `+`, deducing each independently. Before C++14 a lambda's parameter types had to be spelled out concretely.

cards.term: What did C++14 relax about constexpr functions?
cards.definition: C++11 constexpr functions were essentially limited to a single `return` expression. C++14 allowed loops, local variables and multiple statements inside a constexpr function, so far more logic could run at compile time naturally rather than being contorted into one expression.
cards.summary: constexpr functions gained loops, locals and multiple statements.

cards.term:
Exercise: modernise this C++03 loop.
`for (std::vector<int>::iterator it = v.begin(); it != v.end(); ++it) { std::cout << *it; }`
cards.sub: Use two C++11 features to shrink it.
cards.definition: `for (const auto& x : v) { std::cout << x; }`. Range-based for removes the explicit `begin`/`end`/`++it` plumbing, and `auto` removes the verbose iterator type. `const auto&` reads each element without copying and without allowing mutation.
cards.summary: `for (const auto& x : v) std::cout << x;` — range-for + auto.

cards.term:
Exercise: replace this raw allocation with the modern C++14 idiom.
`Widget* w = new Widget(1, 2); ... delete w;`
cards.sub: Combine the right standard's factory function.
cards.definition: `auto w = std::make_unique<Widget>(1, 2);` (C++14). No raw `new`, no matching `delete`, exception-safe, and the `Widget` is freed automatically when `w` leaves scope. This is the canonical modern replacement for owning raw pointers.
